summary: Ronacher argues that programming agents turn long-standing developer pain points into something measurable rather than anecdotal. He observes that teams routinely blame users for failures caused by inadequate tooling, documentation, and API design, but agents expose these weaknesses at scale. By running agents on real tasks, he finds objective differences in languages and ecosystems that go beyond training data, including tooling quality, stability, and usability. He proposes using agents as repeatable, low-emotion evaluators of developer experience, effectively proxying how humans feel when working with a codebase. The conclusion is that healthier codebases and tools will show up as higher agent success, and that teams should use this to guide technology choices and library design.
key_points: Agents make developer experience measurable, revealing tooling and API flaws that are often dismissed as user error.
Language and ecosystem choices should be evaluated not just by AI familiarity but by tooling quality, stability, and inherent capabilities.
Agent frustration and failure rates can serve as objective signals for codebase health and developer productivity.
Specific indicators of good DX include strong tests/examples, clear error reporting, stable ecosystems, fewer abstractions, fast tools, and robust local dev environments.
money_quotes: What agents change about this is, is that I can subject them to something I wouldn't really want to subject other developers to: measuring.
Using agents to measure code quality is great because agents don't judge me, but they do judge the code they are writing.
We should pay more attention to how healthy engineering teams are, and that starts with the code base.
Getting agents that have never seen a codebase start using a library is repeatable, rather cheap, fast and if set up the right way very objective.
Right at this very moment though what programming agents need to be successful is rather well aligned with what humans need.
When an agent struggles, so does a human.
There is a lot of code and tooling out there which is objectively not good, but because of one reason or another became dominant.
key_insight: Agents can act as objective, repeatable proxies for developer experience, turning tooling and ecosystem quality into measurable signals that should guide engineering choices.
